title: Public Administration Paper I — Administrative Theory

qs.q: "Public Administration has never resolved whether its focus is a set of principles or its locus a particular institution; each answer has redrawn the boundaries of the discipline." Comment.
"Wilson founded the discipline upon a dichotomy that his own later career as an administrator belied." Examine the continuing significance of Woodrow Wilson's vision of Public Administration.

"From Minnowbrook I to Minnowbrook III, New Public Administration travelled from relevance, values and social equity to networks, globalisation and technology." Examine the contribution and limitations of NPA to administrative theory.
"Paul Appleby held that government is different in kind, and not merely in degree, from private management." Critically examine the distinctiveness of public administration as a field of activity.
"Public Administration has survived every 'identity crisis' by borrowing a new identity; each crisis has enriched rather than enfeebled the discipline." Comment.
"Good governance is as much about the legitimacy of process as it is about the efficiency of outcome." Comment.

"New Public Management promised efficiency but delivered fragmentation; New Public Governance seeks to restore coherence through collaboration." Critically examine the paradigm shift and its relevance for the state.
"The New Public Service restores the citizen, not the customer, to the centre of administration." Comment.
"Governance is government plus networks, markets and communities." Critically examine the implications of this reconceptualisation for the role and capacity of the state.
"POSDCORB tells us what a chief executive does, but not what administration is for." Comment on the adequacy of Gulick's formulation.
"Urwick sought in the management of men the precision of an engineer." Examine the classical principles of organisation and the criticism that they are but 'proverbs'.
"The classical and the human-relations schools are not rival theories but two halves of a single theory of organisation." Discuss.
"Likert's 'System-4' organisation, held together by 'linking pins', makes participation the very technology of organisational integration." Examine.
"Scientific Management dehumanised the worker in the name of efficiency, yet its logic survives in every performance dashboard of the digital state." Examine.

"Weber's prophecy of the 'iron cage' has come true not through the failure of bureaucracy but through its success." In the light of this statement, examine the continuing relevance of the Weberian ideal type and the post-Weberian developments culminating in the Neo-Weberian State.
"The Hawthorne experiments discovered the 'social man' in the organisation but ignored the structure of power within it." Critically examine.

"There is a fundamental incongruence between the needs of a mature personality and the demands of a formal organisation." Discuss Argyris' proposition and evaluate participative management as its remedy.
"Fayol's principles of administration have weathered time better than Taylor's techniques of work." Do you agree? Justify.
"Mary Parker Follett's 'law of the situation' anticipated the logic of contemporary collaborative governance." Discuss.
"Weber's bureaucracy is an ideal type, not an empirical description of reality." Comment.
"Barnard's 'zone of indifference' explains why authority ultimately flows upward from the subordinate." Examine.
"The Human Relations School humanised the workplace but romanticised the worker." Comment.
"It is the need for achievement rather than the lure of money that distinguishes the high performer." Examine McClelland's theory for public organisations.

"The study of leadership moved from the traits of the leader, through the behaviour of the leader, to the demands of the situation." Examine the contribution of the behavioural theories of leadership, including the Managerial Grid.

"There is no one best style of leadership; effectiveness lies in the match between the leader's style and the demands of the situation." Examine with reference to Fiedler's contingency model and Hersey–Blanchard's situational leadership.
"Vroom's Expectancy theory explains why identical incentives motivate different employees differently." Examine its utility for public organisations.

"Artificial Intelligence offers a paradoxical answer to Simon's 'bounded rationality': machines now extend the limits of rationality even as they narrow the space for human judgement." Examine the implications for the process and techniques of administrative decision-making.
"In the digital workplace, the classical problem of communication has been inverted: from scarcity of information to its overload." Discuss the consequences for organisational communication.
"Simon's concept of 'bounded rationality' is a more honest account of administrative choice than the myth of the 'economic man'." Comment.
"Maslow's need hierarchy is more a philosophy of human nature than a verifiable theory of motivation." Examine.
"Leadership in public organisations is increasingly transformational rather than transactional." Examine the proposition in the context of administrative reform.
"Communication is the nervous system of an organisation, and the grapevine is its informal pulse." Comment.
"Herzberg's hygiene factors only prevent dissatisfaction; they do not motivate." Examine.
"Decision-making is a continuum from programmed to non-programmed, and technology is reshaping both ends." Examine with reference to Simon and contemporary e-governance.
"Morale and motivation are related but distinct; high morale does not guarantee high productivity." Discuss.
"The difference between line and staff agencies is one of function, not of rank." Comment, with reference also to auxiliary agencies.
"The rigid pyramid is yielding to flatter, matrix and networked forms of organisation." Examine the emerging structural forms and their administrative implications.
"The chief executive is the keystone of the administrative arch." Examine the types and functions of the chief executive.

"The public corporation was devised to combine the autonomy of business with the accountability of government; the government company was devised to escape both." Critically examine with reference to the forms of public enterprise.
"Ad hoc and advisory bodies extend the expertise of government but diffuse its responsibility." Comment.
"The systems approach treats the organisation as a living organism that imports, transforms and exports energy." Examine the contribution and limitations of the open-systems view of organisations.
"The distance between regulation and over-regulation is thin." Examine in the context of independent regulatory authorities.
"Contingency theory abandons the search for one best way of organising." Analyse its contribution to organisation theory.
"Public-Private Partnerships blur the boundary between public and private accountability." Comment.
"The relationship between headquarters and the field is one of perpetual tension." Examine.
"Administrative discretion is a necessary evil; only its abuse is an avoidable one." Comment.
"Accountability has travelled from answerability for the use of authority to responsibility for the achievement of results." Critically examine the changing concept of administrative accountability.
"Whistle-blowing sets the conscience of the civil servant against the loyalty demanded by the service." Examine whistle-blower protection as an instrument of accountability.
"Executive control over administration is the most continuous, the most pervasive and the least visible of all controls." Discuss its instruments and its limitations.
"The widening exemption of 'personal information' under the data protection regime risks hollowing out the Right to Information." Critically examine.

"Judicial control over administration has travelled from testing the legality of administrative action to prescribing its very content." Critically examine with reference to the doctrines of proportionality and legitimate expectation.
"Statutory Right-to-Service laws in the States have achieved what three decades of Citizen's Charters could not." Examine.
"Social audit democratises accountability by turning beneficiaries into auditors." Comment.
"The Right to Information shifted the locus of accountability from the state to the citizen." Comment.
"Legislative control over administration has weakened even as judicial and citizen control have strengthened." Critically examine.
"Citizen's Charters promised a service revolution but lapsed into ritualism." Comment.
"The principles of natural justice are the unwritten constitution of administrative adjudication." Examine.

"Administrative tribunals were designed to relieve the ordinary courts, but have often ended up adding one more layer of litigation." Critically examine in the light of judicial pronouncements on tribunal autonomy.
"Dicey's hostility to administrative law was rooted in a misreading of the French droit administratif." Comment.
"The administrative state is constitutional precisely to the extent that it is accountable." Discuss.
"Delegated legislation is the inevitable child of the administrative state; judicial review is its indispensable guardian." Critically examine.
"Administrative systems are the products of their history and their society." Comment with reference to the ecological approach.

"Comparative Public Administration is, in practice, the comparative study of bureaucracies." Critically examine the bureaucracy-centred approach associated with Ferrel Heady, and its adequacy for the discipline today.

"The higher civil service is a career aristocracy in France, a generalist meritocracy in Britain, and a politically permeated service in the United States." Compare, and assess what each model offers to developing countries.
"In a prismatic society, formalism is the measured distance between the prescriptive and the descriptive." Explain with reference to Riggs.

"Pronounced dead in the 1970s, Comparative Public Administration has been given a new lease of life by globalisation, governance indices and digital interdependence." Critically examine the current status of the discipline.
"Riggs' Prismatic-Sala model remains a useful lens for understanding transitional societies despite its pessimism." Comment.
"Comparative Public Administration has moved from the ecological approach to the political-economy approach." Analyse the implications of this shift for the identity of the discipline.
"The ecological approach made the study of administration culture-sensitive." Comment.
"Riggs' prismatic society is criticised as static and pessimistic, yet it endures in comparative analysis." Critically examine.
"Development administration is defined by its goal-orientation and its change-orientation." Examine Weidner's conception.
"A development bureaucracy must be committed to goals, not neutral between them." Examine the 'committed bureaucracy' debate in development administration.
"It is state capacity, not state size, that determines developmental success." Discuss the concept of state capacity and its relevance for developing countries.

"Development is the expansion of substantive freedoms, and not merely the growth of national income." Examine Amartya Sen's reconceptualisation of development and its implications for development administration.
"Development administration has journeyed from nation-building to the localisation of the Sustainable Development Goals." Comment.
"The East Asian developmental state succeeded because its bureaucracy enjoyed 'embedded autonomy'." Examine the relevance of this proposition for developing countries.
"Digital public infrastructure has opened a new frontier for women's economic empowerment, but the gender digital divide threatens to close it." Discuss.
"Women's empowerment is at once a means and an end of development." Discuss.
"The 'strong state versus free market' debate has been overtaken by the idea of the 'developmental state'." Examine.
"The anti-development thesis is a critique of the model of modernity, not of development itself." Comment.
"Development administration has given way to the administration of development." Discuss.
"Self-help groups have feminised the grassroots economy." Examine.
"Sound employer–employee relations in government require institutionalised consultation, not benevolent paternalism." Comment on the machinery of joint consultation.

"The right of civil servants to form associations sits uneasily with the discipline demanded of a public service." Critically examine employer–employee relations and the machinery of joint consultation in government.
"Rank classification honours the person; position classification honours the post." Compare the two systems of classification and assess their suitability for Indian personnel administration.
"Public pay must reconcile the principle of comparability with the capacity of the exchequer." Comment in the context of periodic pay revisions in government.

"The doctrine of neutrality demands anonymity from the civil servant, while the digital age demands visibility from everyone." Critically examine the emerging challenges to civil service neutrality and anonymity.
"The political neutrality of the civil service is a constitutional value under strain." Comment.
"Administrative ethics cannot be legislated; it must be cultivated." Examine in the light of codes of conduct and integrity institutions.
"Training is an investment, not an item of expenditure." Comment.
"Performance appraisal in government is more about compliance than about employee development." Critically examine and suggest reforms.
"Mixed-scanning is a pragmatic marriage of the rational and the incremental models of decision." Examine Etzioni's contribution.
"In a democracy, public policy tends to reflect the preferences of the powerful few more faithfully than the wishes of the many." Examine the elite and pluralist theories of policy-making.
"The tidy 'policy cycle' of the textbooks bears little resemblance to the untidy reality of policy-making." Critically examine the stagist model of the policy process.
"A policy window opens only when the streams of problems, policies and politics converge." Explain Kingdon's framework of agenda-setting.
"Dror's normative-optimum model seeks a middle path between the arrogance of the rationalist and the timidity of the incrementalist." Examine.
"Top-down implementation fails for want of local knowledge; bottom-up implementation fails for want of coherence." Discuss with reference to the approaches to policy implementation.
"Policy evaluation is the weakest link in the policy cycle." Examine.
"Lindblom's incrementalism is descriptively accurate but normatively conservative." Critically examine.
"Evidence-based policy making is constrained more by politics than by the availability of data." Discuss.
"Implementation is where policy meets reality, and most often where it fails." Examine.
"Public policy is the authoritative allocation of values; the real question is whose values get allocated." Comment.
"Interest groups distort the policy agenda in developing democracies." Examine.

"Organisation and Methods was conceived as the built-in self-correcting device of administration." Examine the rise and decline of O&M, and the case for its reincarnation as digital process re-engineering.
"E-governance matures through the stages of presence, interaction, transaction and transformation." Examine the models and stages of e-governance as techniques of administrative improvement.

"Digital Public Infrastructure has transformed e-governance from the computerisation of procedures to the re-imagination of the state–citizen interface." Critically examine with suitable illustrations.
"Network techniques such as PERT and CPM bring rigour to project administration." Comment.
"Artificial Intelligence is transforming the techniques of administrative improvement from descriptive analysis to predictive governance." Examine, with reference to management aids.
"Management Information Systems have evolved from data processing to decision support." Comment.
"Planning-Programming-Budgeting sought to convert the budget into an instrument of rational choice." Comment.
"The budget is a profoundly incremental and political document, not a rational-comprehensive plan." Examine Wildavsky's theory of the budgetary process.
"Audit has evolved from checking the legality of expenditure to appraising the wisdom of policy." Examine the changing scope and types of government audit.
"Gender budgeting is not a separate budget; it is a lens upon the whole budget." Comment.
"Successive budgetary reforms — from performance budgets to outcome budgets — have improved the form of the budget more than its function." Examine.
"Fiscal policy in developing countries perpetually oscillates between the imperative of growth and the discipline of debt." Comment with reference to fiscal responsibility frameworks.
"Zero-based budgeting failed not in concept but in execution." Comment.
"The budget is an instrument of political accountability before it is a tool of financial management." Examine in the light of budgetary reforms.
"Performance budgeting links expenditure to outcomes but struggles with the problem of attribution." Comment.
"Internal audit is the conscience-keeper of financial administration." Discuss.
"Public debt is a bridge between generations as much as a burden upon them." Discuss.

title: Public Administration Paper II — Indian Administration

qs.q: "Cornwallis founded a civil service that was honest but alien." Comment on the Cornwallis reforms and the origins of the covenanted service.
"Indian administration carries the layered imprint of its Mughal-Persian and British inheritances." Comment.
"Dyarchy was an unworkable halfway house, and yet the training ground of Indian self-government." Examine the Montagu–Chelmsford reforms as a stage in the evolution of Indian administration.
"Indianization of the public services was conceded by the colonial state as a political necessity, never as an administrative conviction." Examine with reference to the Islington and Lee Commissions.
"Kautilya's 'Saptanga' theory of the State anticipates the modern idea of the administrative state." Comment.

Trace the constitutional evolution of Indian administration from the Charter Acts to the Government of India Act, 1935, and assess the continuities carried into the constitutional scheme of independent India.
Relevance of Kautilya's Arthashastra as a manual of statecraft and administrative ethics.
"The colonial land-revenue settlements left a lasting imprint on Indian field administration." Examine the legacy of the Permanent, Ryotwari and Mahalwari systems.
The Mansabdari system and land-revenue administration under the Mughals.
"The British bequeathed India a 'steel frame' designed for control rather than development." Critically examine the colonial legacy of district administration and the ICS.
Discuss the continuities and departures between Mughal administrative practice and the modern Indian system.

sections.t: 2. Philosophical and Constitutional Framework of Government

qs.q: "The Directive Principles are the conscience-keeper of Indian public administration." Comment on the value premises of the administrative system.
"Independent India placed the goals of a welfare state upon the shoulders of a colonial steel frame." Critically examine the value premises and the philosophical framework of Indian administration.
"India is a federation designed with a strong unitary bias." Examine the administrative implications of the quasi-federal structure of the Constitution.
"The Preamble is the identity card of the Constitution and the moral compass of Indian administration." Comment.
"The Indian bureaucracy functions within a political culture that prizes hierarchy, patronage and personalised authority." Examine its impact on administrative behaviour and performance.
"Constitutionalism means limited government." Comment on its administrative significance in India.
The influence of political culture on Indian administrative behaviour.
"Bureaucracy and democracy are uneasy but indispensable partners." Examine the tension in India.
"The Memorandum of Understanding promised autonomy without surrendering accountability." Comment on the MoU and the Navratna framework for public enterprises.

"From the commanding heights of the economy to a strategic minimum, the very rationale of the public sector has been reversed." Critically examine the changing role of the public sector and the challenges of its governance.
"Asset monetisation is disinvestment by another name." Examine in the context of the public sector.

"Strategic disinvestment and the policy of retaining a 'bare minimum' presence in strategic sectors have redefined the public sector from the commanding heights of the economy to a portfolio of strategic assets." Critically examine.
Problems of autonomy and accountability of Public Sector Undertakings.
Impact of liberalisation and privatisation on the public sector.
"The real work of parliamentary scrutiny has migrated from the floor of the House to the committee room." Examine the departmentally-related standing committee system.

"The Ministry is a political creation; the Department is its administrative reality." Examine the structure and working of Ministries and Departments of the Government of India in the light of the Allocation of Business and Transaction of Business Rules.
"Cabinet Committees quietly redistribute the collective authority of the Cabinet." Comment.
"The Central Secretariat, conceived as the brain of the Union Government, is faulted for a generalist–specialist imbalance." Critically examine its structure and the case for domain expertise.
"The Cabinet Secretariat is the apex instrument of administrative coordination." Discuss its role and functions.
Distinguish between attached offices, subordinate offices and field organisations, and assess their role in policy implementation.
The Prime Minister's Office (PMO) as the emerging nerve-centre of Union administration.
"Parliamentary oversight of the executive and administration has steadily weakened." Critically examine the executive–legislature relationship in India.
"Judicial review has expanded into the judicial governance of administration." Examine with reference to administrative action.
Discuss the changing character of cabinet government and collective responsibility in the Union executive.
"NITI Aayog governs the States by ranking them rather than by directing them." Comment on competitive federalism through indices.
"A plan is only as good as its last-mile evaluation." Examine India's machinery for programme implementation, monitoring and outcome evaluation.
"The Aspirational Blocks Programme carries the logic of competitive and cooperative federalism below the district." Comment.
"With the Planning Commission gone, State Planning Boards have lost their interlocutor without finding a new role." Examine the machinery of planning at the State level.
"The shift from the Planning Commission to NITI Aayog replaced directive planning with cooperative and competitive federalism." Critically examine the change and its limitations.
Assess NITI Aayog's Aspirational Districts Programme as an instrument of development administration.
"Decentralised planning was to be the cornerstone of participatory development, yet District Planning Committees remain moribund." Critically examine.

"Fiscal transfers have received far more attention than the institutional machinery of Centre–State coordination." Critically examine the role of the Inter-State Council, the Zonal Councils and allied forums in Indian cooperative federalism.

"The Sixteenth Finance Commission signals a shift from entitlement-based transfers to compliance-driven fiscal federalism." Critically examine its recommendations on devolution criteria, local body grants and fiscal discipline.
"Simultaneous elections promise administrative efficiency but test the federal architecture." Critically examine the administrative implications of the 'One Nation, One Election' proposal.

"Through dashboards, delivery units and a strengthened Chief Minister's Office, State governments are replicating the PMO model of concentrated coordination." Examine its implications for the State Secretariat system.

"The Supreme Court's opinion on the Presidential Reference concerning assent to State Bills has redrawn the constitutional space of the Governor's office." Critically examine its implications for Union–State relations and State administration.
"The Goods and Services Tax and successive Finance Commissions are recasting Indian fiscal federalism." Critically examine the changing Union–State financial relations.
Discuss the terms of reference and the challenges before the Sixteenth Finance Commission.
The Governor's discretionary powers and recent controversies.
"The Chief Secretary is the linchpin of state administration." Discuss the role of the Chief Secretary and the State Secretariat.
"The relationship between the Secretariat and the Directorates reflects the classic staff–line tension in state administration." Examine.

"The empowerment of Panchayati Raj calls for the redefinition, not the retention, of the Collector's traditional primacy." Critically examine the role of the Collector in an era of democratic decentralisation.
The changing role of the District Collector from regulation to development.

"From pandemics to floods, the District Collector remains the pivot of crisis administration even as line departments and specialised agencies have multiplied." Critically examine the Collector's coordinating role in disaster management in the light of the amended disaster management framework.

"Digitisation of land records and drone-based property validation are transforming the oldest function of district administration." Examine with reference to recent initiatives in land administration.
Union–State–local relations in contemporary district administration.
"The Union Public Service Commission is the sentinel of merit in the Indian bureaucracy." Comment.
"The Indian civil service suffers less from a deficit of talent than from a deficit of motivation." Examine the challenges of morale and motivation in the higher civil service.
"The provision for compulsory retirement is the exception that proves the rule of a job for life." Examine performance-based accountability in the civil service.
"Article 311 protects the honest civil servant and shelters the errant one alike." Critically examine the constitutional safeguards available to civil servants and the case for their recalibration.

"The All India Services are the federal glue of Indian administration, yet the States increasingly experience them as instruments of the Union." Critically examine, with reference to recent cadre-deputation controversies.
"The Unified Pension Scheme seeks a middle path between the assurance of the old pension and the fiscal sustainability of the new." Comment.
"Recent training reforms treat the civil servant as a lifelong learner rather than a one-time entrant." Comment with reference to the iGOT–Karmayogi ecosystem.
"Lateral entry into the higher civil services has oscillated between the promise of domain expertise and the politics of representation." Critically examine in the light of recent developments.
Mission Karmayogi and competency-based capacity-building in the civil services.
"Civil-service neutrality and civil-service activism embody two rival conceptions of the bureaucrat's role." Critically examine in the Indian context.
"Capacity-building, not merely recruitment reform, holds the key to a future-ready civil service." Discuss.
Examine the challenges of preserving a merit-based and impartial higher civil service amid politicisation.
Grievance-redressal mechanisms in Indian administration (CPGRAMS and Sevottam).
"Good governance is achieved less by creating new institutions than by reforming old ones." Critically examine recent good-governance and civil-service reform initiatives.
Examine the code of conduct, discipline and the question of the political rights of civil servants.
"The Public Financial Management System has made the rupee traceable from the treasury to the beneficiary." Comment on DBT and PFMS.
"The Consolidated Fund, the Contingency Fund and the Public Account together map the constitutional geography of public money." Examine.
"Expenditure reform has consistently lagged behind revenue reform in India." Examine the case for reforming public expenditure management.
"In India the budget is prepared in secrecy, presented with ceremony and passed in haste." Examine the stages of the budgetary process and the scope for its reform.
"Single Nodal Agency and just-in-time release reforms have tightened the Union's grip over scheme finances." Comment.
"The financial committees of Parliament are the 'twin sisters' of the Comptroller and Auditor General, yet the audit-to-accountability chain in India is broken at several links." Critically examine.
The budget as an instrument of political accountability.
"Parliamentary control over public expenditure has become more ritual than real." Critically examine the instruments of financial control.
Discuss the role of the Ministry of Finance in the fiscal and monetary management of the economy.
Parliamentary financial committees: the Public Accounts, Estimates and Public Undertakings Committees.
"The CAG is the constitutional conscience-keeper of public finance, yet its audit is criticised as a post-mortem." Critically examine the role of the Comptroller and Auditor General.
Discuss the role of the Controller General of Accounts and the case for accrual-based accounting in government.

"From Gorwala and Appleby to the two Administrative Reforms Commissions, India has never lacked reform reports — only their implementation." Critically examine the trajectory of administrative reform since Independence.
"The Administrative Reforms Commissions failed less in design than in implementation." Comment.
"Administrative reform in India has moved from structural change to technology-driven process re-engineering." Discuss.

"'Minimum Government, Maximum Governance' is a philosophy of administrative reform rather than a programme of retrenchment." Examine with reference to deregulation, decriminalisation of minor offences and process re-engineering.
"The block, and not the district, is the true fulcrum of rural development administration." Examine the institutional machinery of rural development.

"The Community Development Programme failed as a programme but succeeded as a lesson: it gave India Panchayati Raj." Examine the legacy of the CDP and NES in the light of the Balwant Rai Mehta Committee report.
"The transition from MGNREGA to a successor employment-guarantee framework marks a redesign of the rural safety net." Comment.
"From bank linkage to 'Lakhpati Didi', the Self-Help Group movement has become the principal instrument of rural livelihood promotion." Critically examine.
"PESA promised the tribal Gram Sabha a veto, but delivered it a footnote." Comment.
The 73rd Constitutional Amendment and the 'three Fs' of devolution.
"The Indian bureaucracy was structured for regulation but conscripted for development." Examine the tension between order-maintenance and development.
MGNREGA as a rights-based rural livelihood guarantee.
"Rural development programmes have multiplied, but convergence and last-mile delivery remain elusive." Critically examine India's rural development strategy since Independence.
"The metropolis is planned by everyone and governed by no one." Comment on metropolitan governance and the Metropolitan Planning Committee.
"Globalisation has made cities the new theatres of governance, straining the 'local' against the 'global'." Examine the global-local debate and the idea of 'new localism' in urban governance.

"In Indian city government, the executive belongs to the State and the deliberative to the city." Examine the Municipal Commissioner–elected council relationship and its implications for accountable city management.

"Local bodies in India suffer less from a shortage of funds than from a shortage of the will to raise them." Critically examine the problem of own-source revenue in the light of the entry-level conditions and performance grants recommended by the Sixteenth Finance Commission.

"Recurrent urban flooding is a failure of urban governance more than a fury of nature." Examine with reference to institutional fragmentation in metropolitan planning and the imperatives of climate-resilient cities.
"Municipal finance is the Achilles' heel of urban governance." Examine with reference to the 74th Amendment and missions such as AMRUT and the Smart Cities Mission.
"Indian policing still wears a uniform stitched in 1861." Comment on the colonial legacy of police administration.

"The Police Act of 1861 was written to secure the ruler, not to serve the citizen; its unfinished replacement is the story of police reform in India." Critically examine the British legacy of police administration and the agenda for its reform.
"The new criminal procedure code re-engineers policing around statutory timelines and technology." Comment.

"Police reform in India now runs on two tracks: judicially mandated structural reform and legislatively mandated procedural reform." Examine with reference to the Prakash Singh directives and the new criminal laws.
Police-public relations and community policing.
"Police reform in India has become a matter of litigation rather than legislation." Examine in the light of the National Police Commission and the Prakash Singh judgment (2006).
Criminalisation of politics and its impact on administration.
Examine the role of central investigative and para-military agencies in countering insurgency and terrorism, and the federal concerns they raise.

"The proliferation of national commissions for the disadvantaged has multiplied advocacy without guaranteeing accountability." Critically examine the role and effectiveness of the national commissions for the Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, Backward Classes and Women.
"The largest arrear in Indian governance is the arrear of justice." Examine the crisis of judicial administration and the reforms addressing it.
"India's anti-corruption architecture suffers from overlapping jurisdiction and underlapping autonomy." Critically examine with reference to the CVC, the CBI and the Lokayuktas.
"The amended disaster management law consolidates planning authority at the apex, while disaster response remains an intensely local act." Critically examine this centralisation–localisation tension.
"The regulatory tightening of foreign contributions has redrawn the boundary between the state and civil society." Examine its implications for voluntary organisations in development administration.
The Lokpal: promise and performance as an anti-corruption institution.
"Regulatory commissions are the new frontier of the administrative state." Examine their role and the threats to their autonomy.
The Disaster Management Act, 2005 and the role of the NDMA.
"The National Human Rights Commission is a recommendatory body without teeth." Examine its role in administrative accountability.

sections.t: 1. Salient Features of World Physical Geography

qs.q: The Indian monsoon is a complex atmospheric phenomenon driven by differential heating, pressure gradients, and upper-air circulation. Discuss the mechanism of the Indian monsoon system, explaining the roles of the Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ), the subtropical jet stream, and the Somali Current in determining its onset, progression, and withdrawal.

Contrast the characteristics of Himalayan and Peninsular river systems in India. How do their geological origins, flow patterns, and hydrological regimes influence water resource availability and management in the regions they traverse?

Discuss the major forest types of India as classified by Champion and Seth, and examine the factors — climatic, edaphic, and anthropogenic — that determine their distribution. How does deforestation threaten India's biodiversity and ecosystem services?

Describe the major features of the ocean floor — continental shelf, continental slope, abyssal plains, mid-ocean ridges, and deep-sea trenches. How does India's extensive continental shelf influence its resource potential and maritime jurisdiction?
Explain the mechanism of the Indian monsoon system. Discuss the factors responsible for its inter-annual variability and the implications of this variability for Indian agriculture.
Discuss the theory of plate tectonics. How does it account for the major geomorphic features of the Earth, including the formation of mountain ranges and ocean basins?

"The Himalayas are not a static physical barrier but a dynamic and evolving mountain system." Discuss the geological processes responsible for their continued uplift and the associated natural hazards.
What is an 'atmospheric river'? Discuss its role in causing extreme rainfall events and examine the adequacy of India's early-warning systems for such events.
Explain the mechanism of the La Niña phenomenon and discuss its implications for monsoon patterns and disaster risk in South and Southeast Asia.

El Niño events are becoming more frequent and intense in a warming world. Analyse the specific mechanisms through which El Niño affects India's monsoon variability, and evaluate its cascading impact on agricultural output and food security with reference to recent episodes.
How does the Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) influence the Indian monsoon? Why is understanding IOD important for India's agricultural planning and disaster preparedness?

Western Disturbances have become increasingly erratic in recent years, causing unseasonal rainfall and crop damage across northern India. Explain the mechanism of Western Disturbances and discuss their changing behaviour in the context of global warming.
Factors Responsible for Formation and Global Distribution of Hot Deserts

sections.t: 2. Distribution of Key Natural Resources & Factors for Industry Location

qs.q: The distribution of key mineral and energy resources across the world is highly uneven, and this unevenness has significant geopolitical implications. Discuss the global and Indian distribution patterns of major minerals and energy resources, and analyse how resource geography shapes industrial location and international trade.

India's diverse physiography has produced a variety of soil types, each with distinct characteristics that determine agricultural productivity. Discuss the major soil types of India, their geographical distribution, and the challenges of soil degradation that threaten India's food security.

"Critical minerals have emerged as the new frontier of global economic geopolitics." Discuss the distribution of critical mineral resources in and around India, and examine the strategic significance of recent international cooperation frameworks in this regard.

"India's renewable energy ambitions are constrained as much by geography as by technology." Discuss the regional distribution of solar and wind energy potential in India and the challenges in harnessing this potential.

"The suspension of a major trans-boundary water-sharing treaty has brought the geography of South Asian river basins into sharp focus." Discuss the physiographic and hydrological characteristics of the Indus river system and the implications of changes in water-sharing arrangements for the region.
Examine the factors influencing the location of the iron and steel industry, with reference to the changing significance of raw-material linkages in the era of globalization.
Discuss the major inter-state river water disputes in India and examine the geographical and political factors that complicate their resolution.
Major Soil Types of India and Their Relationship with Agricultural Land-Use Patterns
Global Distribution of Major Fishing Grounds and Factors Responsible for Their Productivity
Spatial Pattern of Agricultural Land Use in India and Regional Variations in Cropping Intensity

The geopolitics of critical minerals has emerged as a defining feature of 21st-century resource competition. Discuss the global distribution of critical minerals, India's vulnerabilities arising from import dependence, and the strategic measures adopted under the National Critical Minerals Mission to secure supply chains.

India's Deep Ocean Mission aims to position the country as a leader in sustainable deep-sea resource exploration. Discuss the strategic significance of polymetallic nodules in the Central Indian Ocean Basin, and evaluate the technological and environmental challenges of deep-sea mining.

India extracts more groundwater than any other country in the world, yet several river basins are approaching water-scarce status. Discuss the geographical pattern of India's groundwater crisis with reference to its impact on agriculture and drinking water security.
Why is the Arctic region gaining increasing geopolitical significance? Discuss India's strategic interests in the Arctic with reference to the Northern Sea Route and critical mineral deposits.

Discuss the geographical factors influencing the location of India's solar and wind energy installations. How is the renewable energy landscape reshaping India's economic geography and energy security?

Industrial corridors have been envisaged as engines of India's economic transformation. Discuss the locational rationale behind India's major industrial corridors and evaluate their potential to reshape the country's industrial geography, including the emerging semiconductor and green energy clusters.
Critically examine the feasibility and environmental implications of the Interlinking of Rivers programme in India as a solution to the country's water distribution challenges.

The mismatch between cropping patterns and agro-climatic zones in India has led to unsustainable water use and declining soil health. Discuss the geographical basis for this mismatch and suggest measures for climate-resilient agricultural planning.

sections.t: 3. Important Geophysical Phenomena (Earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic Activity, Cyclones, etc.)

qs.q: India lies at the junction of multiple tectonic plates, making several of its regions highly vulnerable to earthquakes. Discuss the plate tectonic setting of the Indian subcontinent and examine the seismic zonation of India with reference to the distribution of earthquake-prone areas.

"The North Indian Ocean witnessed one of its deadliest and costliest cyclone seasons in 2025." Discuss the factors responsible for the increasing frequency and intensity of post-monsoon cyclones in the Bay of Bengal, and examine India's disaster-preparedness mechanisms in this regard.

Account for the increasing seismic activity felt across northern India in recent times. Discuss the geological factors responsible for the seismic vulnerability of the Himalayan and trans-Himalayan regions.

"Heatwaves are emerging as a 'silent disaster' with significant socio-economic costs." Discuss the factors responsible for increasing heatwave frequency and intensity in India, and examine the effectiveness of Heat Action Plans.
Discuss the formation and classification of tropical cyclones. Why is the Bay of Bengal more cyclone-prone than the Arabian Sea?
"Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) pose a growing threat to Himalayan states." Explain the mechanism of GLOFs and discuss their implications for disaster management in mountainous regions.
Thunderstorms and Lightning: Phenomenon and Disproportionate Fatalities in Eastern and North-Eastern India

India has been experiencing unprecedented heatwaves with increasing frequency and intensity. Discuss the geographical and anthropogenic factors responsible for the rising incidence of heatwaves, and analyse their cascading impact on urban water availability, public health, and agricultural productivity.

Himalayan glacial retreat is accelerating at an alarming rate, with increasing incidents of Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) and land subsidence in fragile mountain towns. Discuss the geographical causes of these phenomena and their socio-economic consequences for India's river-dependent populations.
What are the causes and consequences of increasing landslide vulnerability in the Western Ghats? Discuss with reference to recent catastrophic events.

Rising sea surface temperatures are linked to increasing frequency and intensity of tropical cyclones in the North Indian Ocean. Discuss the mechanism of this linkage and evaluate the effectiveness of India's cyclone early-warning and disaster preparedness systems.

sections.t: 4. Geographical Features and Their Location — Changes in Critical Geographical Features (Water Bodies, Ice-Caps, Flora & Fauna) and Effects of Such Changes

qs.q: Examine the phenomenon of urban flooding in Indian cities. Discuss the role of unplanned urbanization and inadequate drainage infrastructure in exacerbating this hazard.
Coral Reef Formation and Threats from Climate Change
Examine the factors responsible for increasing incidence of urban flooding in Indian cities, with special reference to the role of changing rainfall patterns and unplanned urbanization.

India's coastal zone supports a significant proportion of its population and economic activity, yet nearly 40% of the coastline is experiencing erosion. Analyse the physical and anthropogenic causes of coastal erosion and discuss the implications for India's Blue Economy aspirations.
How can GIS, remote sensing, and satellite-based technologies enhance India's disaster early-warning systems and climate monitoring capabilities? Illustrate with recent examples.

Post-pandemic India has witnessed a significant shift in internal migration patterns, including reverse migration and the rise of Tier-2 cities as employment destinations. Analyse the geographical and socio-economic factors driving these changes and their implications for India's urban planning.
Examine the causes and consequences of increasing desertification in the arid and semi-arid regions of India. How does climate change exacerbate land degradation in these regions?

sections.t: 1. Indian Culture — Art Forms, Literature and Architecture (Ancient to Modern)

qs.q: The year 2026 marks the 500th anniversary of the establishment of the Mughal Empire in India. Discuss the Mughals' contribution to Indian art, architecture, and composite cultural heritage, and assess their enduring impact on India's civilisational identity.
Discuss how Mauryan art and architecture, particularly the Ashokan pillars and edicts, reflected the ideals of statecraft, moral governance, and the propagation of Dhamma.

The rock-cut cave temples of India represent an extraordinary fusion of religious devotion, artistic expression, and engineering ingenuity. Discuss with reference to the major cave complexes of western and eastern India.
Evaluate the contributions of the Vijayanagara Empire to Indian art, architecture, and cultural synthesis, with special reference to the Hampi monuments.

Indian literature, from Sangam poetry to modern regional literature, has been a powerful medium for social commentary and cultural expression. Discuss the evolution of India's literary traditions and their contribution to national consciousness.

The Bhakti and Sufi movements were not merely religious phenomena but powerful agents of social transformation and cultural synthesis. Discuss their role in bridging social divides and enriching India's composite culture.

Indian textile traditions have been a hallmark of civilizational continuity and cultural identity from the Indus Valley to the present day. Discuss the evolution of major Indian textile traditions and their significance in India's cultural heritage.
"The recently inscribed Maratha Military Landscapes represent a unique synthesis of geo-strategic vision and architectural innovation." Discuss the salient features of Maratha hill-fort architecture.
Discuss the objectives and significance of the Gyan Bharatam Mission in reviving India's manuscript heritage. Examine its role in promoting the Indian Knowledge System (IKS).
Examine the contribution of the Ahom dynasty to the art, architecture and administrative culture of medieval Assam, with particular reference to the Moidams.
Discuss the regional variations that developed in Indo-Islamic architecture under the different Sultanates of medieval India.
"Continuous habitation sites offer a unique window into India's civilizational continuity." Discuss this idea with reference to recent archaeological findings in India.
Trace the evolution of the Nagara style of temple architecture from the Gupta period to the medieval period, highlighting the continuities and changes.
Discuss the salient features of Vijayanagara temple architecture and its contribution to the visual arts of South India.
Salient Features of Buddhist Stupa Architecture in India
Indian Miniature Painting Traditions: Creative Synthesis in the Mughal and Rajput Schools
Modern Indian Art: The Bengal School and the Dialogue Between Indigenous Tradition and Western Influence
Rock-Cut Cave Architecture in India: From the Mauryan to the Gupta Period
Classical Dance and Music Traditions as Intangible Cultural Heritage: Role and Significance of UNESCO's Representative List

sections.t: 2. Modern Indian History (mid-18th century to independence) — Events, Personalities, Issues

qs.q: Critically analyse how British economic policies transformed India's traditional production systems, disrupted indigenous industries, and restructured the Indian economy to serve imperial interests.

Examine the role of the Indian press in shaping public opinion, articulating nationalist aspirations, and mobilizing masses during the colonial period, with reference to key publications and press legislations.

Peasant and agrarian movements formed a significant but often overlooked stream of anti-colonial resistance in India. Discuss the major peasant movements and their contribution to the broader national movement.
Critically examine the causes of the Revolt of 1857 and assess why, despite its widespread nature, it failed to dislodge British rule from India.
Examine the socio-economic impact of the commercialization of agriculture in colonial India.
The 'Drain of Wealth' Theory: Economic Critique of Colonialism and Its Role in the Freedom Movement

sections.t: 3. The Freedom Struggle — Stages and Contributors

qs.q: The Indian freedom struggle was enriched by multiple ideological streams — from moderate constitutionalism to revolutionary nationalism. Compare and contrast the methods and contributions of the revolutionary nationalists with the Gandhian approach to India's independence.

The 19th century witnessed a remarkable ferment of social reform movements across India that addressed caste oppression, gender discrimination, and religious orthodoxy. Critically examine the methods, achievements, and limitations of these reform movements and their continuing relevance.
Discuss the contributions of women reformers and women's organisations in challenging patriarchal structures and shaping modern India, from the colonial period to the early decades of independence.
Discuss how the Quit India Movement of 1942 marked a decisive shift in India's freedom struggle, both in terms of mass participation and the British reassessment of colonial rule in India.
"The Royal Indian Navy Mutiny and the INA trials created conditions that hastened the end of British rule." Discuss the significance of these events in the final phase of India's freedom struggle.
"Peasant and tribal uprisings in colonial India were as significant a part of the national movement as the organized political struggle." Discuss with suitable examples.
"The Swadeshi Movement was as much an economic protest as a political one." Discuss in the context of the Partition of Bengal (1905).
Discuss the role of women in different phases of the Indian national movement, with suitable examples.
Role of the Indian Press in Shaping Nationalist Consciousness During the Freedom Struggle
Revolutionary Nationalism in the Indian Freedom Struggle: Bengal and Punjab

The linguistic reorganization of states in post-independence India was both a democratic concession and an act of nation-building. Discuss its process, challenges, and enduring impact on Indian federalism.

Evaluate the impact of land reforms in post-independence India on rural social structure, agricultural productivity, and the empowerment of marginalised communities. Why did land reforms achieve uneven success across states?

Discuss the recent developments concerning the political-administrative reorganization of North-East India, with reference to the Frontier Nagaland Territorial Authority. Examine the historical context of such demands in the region.
Discuss the process and challenges involved in the integration of Hyderabad and Junagadh into the Indian Union after independence.
"The States Reorganisation Act, 1956 was a landmark attempt to reconcile linguistic diversity with national unity." Discuss.
India's Non-Aligned Foreign Policy: Evolution in the Early Post-Independence Years and Relevance in the Multipolar World

sections.t: 5. History of the World (18th century onward)

qs.q: Decolonization after the Second World War reshaped the political geography of Asia and Africa. Discuss the internal and external factors that catalysed the decolonization process and evaluate how newly independent nations navigated the challenges of state-building.

The Cold War was not merely a military standoff but a contest of development models and ideological visions. Examine its impact on newly independent nations, particularly in shaping their economic strategies and foreign policies.

The post-World War I peace settlements, particularly the Treaty of Versailles, redrew national boundaries but sowed the seeds of future instability. Critically examine how these settlements shaped the political trajectory of the 20th century.

The 19th century witnessed the rise of nationalism that led to the unification of Italy and Germany while simultaneously triggering the disintegration of multi-ethnic empires. Discuss the contrasting outcomes of nationalist movements in 19th-century Europe.

"The American Revolution was not merely a colonial revolt but a defining moment in the global history of democratic ideas." Discuss its significance on the occasion of the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence.
Examine the causes and consequences of the Russian Revolution of 1917 and its impact on the course of twentieth-century world history.
"Decolonization in Asia and Africa after the Second World War reshaped the global political order." Discuss the factors responsible for the wave of decolonization.
Discuss the rise of Fascism and Nazism in Europe between the two World Wars and examine its consequences for the post-war world order.
Post-WWII Boundary Redrawing and Lasting Geopolitical Fault Lines

sections.t: 1. Indian Constitution — Historical Underpinnings, Evolution, Features, Basic Structure, Amendments

qs.q: The Basic Structure Doctrine has been the most consequential judicial innovation in Indian constitutional law. Trace its evolution from Shankari Prasad to Kesavananda Bharati and beyond, and discuss its role in maintaining the supremacy of the Constitution while permitting democratic change.
Constitutional Morality: Doctrine, Application, and Limits

The Constitution (One Hundred and Thirty-first Amendment) Bill sought to alter the basis on which Lok Sabha seats are allocated to States. Discuss the constitutional procedure prescribed under Article 368 for such amendments, and examine why concerns of federal balance are treated as attracting the special-majority-plus-ratification route.

"The freeze on the total number of seats in the Lok Sabha, extended by the 84th Amendment, was intended to reward demographic restraint." Discuss the constitutional and political considerations involved in delinking delimitation from a future census.

The proposed delimitation of Lok Sabha constituencies based on updated population data has raised concerns about the balance of political representation between northern and southern states. Examine the constitutional provisions governing delimitation and discuss its implications for Indian federalism and cooperative Centre-State relations.
Delimitation: Constitutional Framework and Federal Equity

The Constitution (One Hundred and Sixth Amendment) Act, 2023, provides for one-third reservation for women in the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies. Examine the constitutional provisions of this amendment and discuss the challenges in its effective implementation.

The 50th anniversary of the proclamation of Emergency in 1975 has revived debates about the adequacy of constitutional safeguards against the misuse of emergency powers. Discuss the reforms introduced by the 44th Constitutional Amendment Act and evaluate whether they are sufficient to prevent a recurrence.
Emergency Provisions: Constitutional Design, Safeguards, and the 1975 Legacy

The abrogation of Article 370 and the reorganisation of the erstwhile state of Jammu and Kashmir into Union Territories represented a fundamental constitutional transformation. Examine the constitutional validity of this reorganisation and its implications for India's federal structure.

sections.t: 2. Federal Structure — Centre-State Relations, Devolution of Powers & Finances

qs.q: The Supreme Court's advisory opinion in the 16th Presidential Reference held that the discretion of the Governor and President under Articles 200 and 201 is not justiciable, yet "prolonged, unexplained and indefinite inaction" invites limited judicial scrutiny. Examine the constitutional balance this opinion strikes between gubernatorial discretion and legislative supremacy of the States.

"Deemed assent" was rejected by the Supreme Court as "a takeover of the role of a separate constitutional authority." Discuss the constitutional basis of this reasoning and its implications for resolving future Centre-State legislative deadlocks.

The role of the Governor in India has been a subject of persistent constitutional controversy, particularly regarding the exercise of discretionary powers over state legislation. In light of recent Supreme Court observations, critically examine the scope and limits of the Governor's powers under Articles 200 and 201.
The Governor's Constitutional Position and the 16th Presidential Reference

Examine the shift from "entitlement-based" to "compliance-driven" fiscal federalism reflected in the recommendations of the 16th Finance Commission. How does the growing reliance on cesses and surcharges affect the constitutional scheme of tax devolution under Article 270?

Discuss the composition and functions of the Finance Commission under Article 280. To what extent do the horizontal devolution criteria adopted by successive Finance Commissions reconcile the principles of equity and efficiency among States?

The Finance Commission plays a crucial role in maintaining fiscal balance in India's federal structure. Examine the evolving mandate of the Finance Commission and discuss the challenges it faces in ensuring equitable resource distribution between the Centre and States.
16th Finance Commission: Horizontal Devolution and Federal Equity

Examine the GST Council as an exercise in "cooperative federalism" under Article 279A. In light of recent disputes over compensation, cess extension, and vertical devolution, assess whether fiscal federalism in India is trending towards greater centralisation.
GST and Cooperative Federalism: Impact and Reform Agenda

Cooperative federalism has been the stated goal of India's governance framework, yet recent developments suggest increasing friction between the Centre and States. Discuss the institutional mechanisms available for Centre-State coordination and evaluate their effectiveness in resolving intergovernmental disputes.
Inter-State River Water Disputes: Federal Architecture and Tribunal Effectiveness

sections.t: 3. Separation of Powers, Dispute Redressal Institutions & Comparison of Constitutions

qs.q: Compare the mechanisms of judicial review of executive/legislative action available in India and the United Kingdom. In what ways does India's written Constitution with a codified basic structure doctrine mark a departure from the British principle of parliamentary sovereignty?

India's parliamentary democracy draws from the British Westminster model yet has developed distinct features suited to its federal and diverse character. Compare the parliamentary systems of India and the United Kingdom, highlighting the key departures and their constitutional rationale.

"Judicial legislation, through directions issued in public interest litigation, is often criticised as antithetical to separation of powers, yet remains indispensable given legislative and executive inertia." Critically examine this observation with reference to recent instances of the Supreme Court issuing binding directions on matters of governance.

Judicial activism through Public Interest Litigation has been both celebrated as the judiciary's contribution to social justice and criticised as an overreach into the domains of the executive and legislature. Critically examine the evolution, achievements, and limits of PIL in India.
Public Interest Litigation: Transformative Potential and Need for Reform
Article 142 Jurisdiction: Complete Justice or Judicial Overreach?

Administrative tribunals were established to provide specialised and expeditious justice, yet their functioning has been a subject of ongoing constitutional litigation. Examine the constitutional basis and challenges of the tribunal system in India.

Discuss the constitutional provisions governing disqualification of legislators under the Tenth Schedule. Assess whether the discretion vested in the Speaker as a quasi-judicial authority in defection cases is compatible with principles of natural justice, especially given repeated instances of delayed adjudication.

The Anti-Defection Law under the Tenth Schedule was enacted to curb political defections, yet it continues to face criticism regarding its effectiveness and the role of the Speaker in adjudicating defection cases. Critically examine the functioning of the Anti-Defection Law in light of recent judicial pronouncements and political developments.
Anti-Defection Law: Tenth Schedule and Structural Reforms

"Parliamentary Committees are a vital instrument of accountability, yet their recommendations remain largely non-binding." Examine the structure and functioning of the Committee system in ensuring effective oversight of the Executive, with special reference to Financial Committees.
Parliamentary Committees: Role, Decline, and Reform

The declining number of parliamentary sitting days and the increasing resort to ordinances have raised concerns about the erosion of parliamentary sovereignty. Examine the causes and consequences of this trend and suggest measures to strengthen parliamentary accountability.

The Rajya Sabha was conceived as the federal chamber to protect the interests of states in the legislative process. Examine its constitutional role, special powers, and evaluate whether it has effectively functioned as a council of states in practice.

Parliamentary privileges are essential for the effective functioning of legislatures, yet their scope and limits remain a subject of debate. Discuss the constitutional provisions governing parliamentary privileges in India and examine the relationship between parliamentary privilege and fundamental rights.
Leader of the Opposition: Constitutional Significance and Statutory Framework

The proposal for simultaneous elections to the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies has been debated extensively. Examine the constitutional, federal, and practical challenges of implementing 'One Nation, One Election' in India.
One Nation One Election: Constitutional Requirements and Democratic Accountability

The Supreme Court delivered a split verdict on the constitutionality of Section 17A of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988, which mandates prior sanction to investigate public servants. Critically examine the competing constitutional values of executive accountability and protection from vexatious prosecution that underlie this debate.

Discuss the evolving debate on introducing greater transparency in the collegium system of judicial appointments in India. Would a memorandum of procedure with codified criteria compromise judicial independence?
The Collegium System: Critique and Constitutional Imperatives for Reform

The writ jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under Article 32 and of the High Courts under Article 226 forms the cornerstone of the enforcement of fundamental rights in India. Compare and contrast these two jurisdictions and discuss their significance in ensuring the rule of law.

The relationship between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles of State Policy has evolved through a series of landmark judicial pronouncements. Discuss the doctrine of harmonious construction and evaluate whether the current framework achieves the Constitution's vision of social justice without compromising individual liberty.

"The horizons of the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21 continue to expand through judicial interpretation." Illustrate with reference to recent Supreme Court rulings extending Article 21 protections to non-traditional claims of dignity and access.

Discuss the constitutional vision of secularism as reflected in the Preamble and relevant provisions of Part III. How does India's model of principled distance differ from the strict separation model followed in other democracies, and how is this tested by recent legislative and judicial developments concerning religious endowments?
Essential Religious Practices Doctrine and India's Constitutional Secularism

Article 44 of the Constitution directs the State to secure a Uniform Civil Code for citizens throughout the territory of India. Discuss the constitutional, judicial, and socio-political dimensions of implementing a UCC in India's pluralistic society.

The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, seeks to operationalise the right to privacy recognised in Puttaswamy. Examine the key provisions of the Act and discuss whether it adequately balances individual privacy rights with the imperatives of national security and digital governance.
Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023: Privacy Governance Architecture

Freedom of speech and expression under Article 19(1)(a) is the foundation of a democratic society, yet it is subject to reasonable restrictions. Examine the evolving jurisprudence on free speech in India with reference to the challenges posed by social media and digital platforms.
Social Media Regulation: Free Speech, Platform Accountability, and Governance
Sedition Law in India: Constitutional Validity and the BNS Successor
Preventive Detention: Constitutional Framework and Compatibility with Article 21
Custodial Violence: Constitutional Safeguards and Institutional Reform
Death Penalty: Constitutional Validity and Rarest of Rare Doctrine
Citizenship Amendment Act and NRC: Constitutional Issues and Democratic Implications

Pressure groups and civil society organisations play a significant role in shaping public policy and ensuring governmental accountability in India. Examine their role and discuss the challenges they face in the contemporary political environment.
Civil Society: Regulatory Environment and Democratic Governance

sections.t: 9. Representation of the People Act & Electoral Process

qs.q: Discuss the constitutional and statutory basis of the Election Commission's power to conduct a Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls under Article 324 and Section 21 of the Representation of the People Act, 1950. Does this power admit of any judicially enforceable limits?

Examine the doctrinal basis on which the right to vote has been characterised by courts as a constitutional/statutory right rather than a fundamental right. What safeguards exist against arbitrary exclusion from electoral rolls?

Discuss the process of appointment of the Chief Election Commissioner and Election Commissioners under the Chief Election Commissioner and Other Election Commissioners Act, 2023. Does the composition of the Selection Committee adequately safeguard the independence of the Election Commission envisaged under Article 324?

The independence of the Election Commission of India is foundational to the credibility of Indian democracy. Examine the constitutional and statutory framework governing the appointment and functioning of the ECI, and evaluate recent reforms and judicial interventions aimed at strengthening its autonomy.

The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) is described as the 'guardian of the public purse' and plays a vital role in ensuring financial accountability. Discuss the powers and functions of the CAG and evaluate the challenges in maintaining the effectiveness and independence of this institution.
CAG: Constitutional Watchdog in the Era of PPPs and Off-Budget Financing
Lokpal: Design, Performance, and Reform
National Human Rights Commission: Functioning and Reform
Right to Information Act: Effectiveness and Institutional Challenges

sections.t: 11. Local Government (Panchayati Raj & Urban Local Bodies)

qs.q: "The 16th Finance Commission has shifted local body grants from an entitlement to a conditionality-based framework." Discuss the implications of tying local body grants to constitution of Panchayats/Municipalities, timely State Finance Commission formation, and audited-account disclosure, for the goals of the 73rd and 74th Amendments.

"Functions, Functionaries and Funds have given way to the challenge of Functionality." In this context, assess whether fiscal conditionalities strengthen or further constrain the autonomy of local self-government institutions in India.

Despite the constitutional mandate of the 73rd and 74th Amendment Acts, local self-governance institutions in India remain functionally and financially weak. Examine the reasons for the limited devolution of powers to local bodies and suggest measures to strengthen grassroots democracy.
Panchayati Raj: Constitutional Mandate vs. Institutional Reality
Urban Local Bodies: 74th Amendment and the Challenge of Urban Self-Governance

sections.t: 12. Other Constitutional Issues (Schedules, Citizenship & Special Provisions)

qs.q: The Fifth and Sixth Schedules of the Constitution provide special governance frameworks for tribal areas. Compare their provisions, examine their effectiveness in protecting tribal rights and autonomy, and suggest reforms for strengthening tribal self-governance.

The Constitution of India provides for a single citizenship, yet the framework governing acquisition and loss of citizenship has been subject to significant legislative and judicial evolution. Discuss the constitutional and statutory provisions governing Indian citizenship and examine the challenges posed by recent amendments.

The consolidation of twenty-nine central labour laws into four Labour Codes marks a major governance reform. Examine its potential to balance ease of doing business with the social security of the workforce.

The JAM (Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile) trinity and Direct Benefit Transfer have been described as transformative innovations in welfare delivery. Critically evaluate the impact of DBT on reducing leakages, enhancing transparency, and ensuring last-mile delivery of government benefits, while identifying the challenges that persist.

Effective policing is foundational to good governance and the protection of citizens' rights. Discuss the key recommendations for police reform in India and evaluate why implementation has remained elusive despite judicial and executive directives.
India's Cyber Security Governance: Architecture, Gaps, and Reform

Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) has transformed e-governance from a mode of service delivery into a foundational layer of the economy and society. Examine how India's DPI model advances inclusion, while identifying the risks of exclusion it must guard against.

The deployment of Artificial Intelligence in public administration promises efficiency but raises concerns of opacity, bias and diffused accountability. Discuss the safeguards necessary for the responsible use of AI in governance.

Artificial Intelligence and emerging technologies are increasingly being integrated into governance systems to improve service delivery, decision-making, and citizen engagement. Discuss the potential and challenges of AI-driven governance in India, with special reference to the risks of digital exclusion and algorithmic bias.
E-Governance and Digital Exclusion: Building an Inclusive Digital State

Effective grievance redressal lies at the heart of citizen-centric administration. Assess the role of technology-driven grievance redressal mechanisms in bridging the trust deficit between citizens and the State.
Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023: Privacy Governance Architecture

The Right to Information regime and the emerging data-protection framework embody a tension between transparency and privacy. Discuss how this tension can be reconciled without diluting the citizen's right to information.

The Right to Information Act, 2005, was a landmark legislation for ensuring transparency and accountability in governance. Twenty years after its enactment, evaluate the Act's achievements and limitations, and discuss the emerging challenges to its effective implementation.
Right to Information Act: Effectiveness and Institutional Challenges

sections.t: 4. Role of Civil Services in a Democracy

qs.q: Mission Karmayogi seeks to transform the civil services from a rule-based to a role-based framework. Critically examine whether such capacity-building initiatives can address the structural rigidities of the Indian bureaucracy.

The lateral entry scheme in civil services has sparked a vigorous debate about the balance between domain expertise and the generalist administrative tradition. Critically examine the merits and challenges of lateral entry and discuss whether it can coexist with the traditional civil services framework to improve governance outcomes.
UPSC at 100: Civil Services Examination Reform

The proliferation of sector regulators has not always been matched by their functional autonomy. In the context of the newly established data-protection architecture, discuss the challenges to ensuring the independence of regulatory bodies in India.
India's Regulatory Architecture: Design Principles and Reform
National Human Rights Commission: Functioning and Reform

sections.t: 6. Development Processes & the Development Industry (NGOs, SHGs, Civil Society)

qs.q: Self-Help Groups have evolved from thrift-and-credit collectives into agents of rural transformation. Examine their role in advancing women's economic empowerment, and the constraints that limit their scaling up.

Self-Help Groups have been instrumental in empowering rural women economically and socially, with over 10 crore women now part of 91 lakh SHGs under the National Rural Livelihoods Mission. Evaluate the effectiveness of the SHG movement in advancing women's empowerment and discuss the challenges in scaling its impact.

Civil society organisations occupy a contested space between partnership with, and scrutiny by, the State. Critically examine whether the current regulatory framework for foreign-funded NGOs strikes an appropriate balance between accountability and autonomy.

Non-governmental organisations play a vital role in India's development landscape, yet they operate in an increasingly complex regulatory environment. Discuss the challenges NGOs face under the current regulatory framework and evaluate whether the balance between accountability and operational freedom has been appropriately struck.
Civil Society: Regulatory Environment and Democratic Governance

Genuine decentralisation requires the devolution not merely of functions, but also of finances and functionaries. Critically examine the extent to which Panchayati Raj Institutions have been empowered as institutions of self-government.

Effective decentralisation requires that decision-making, resource allocation, and implementation capacities are located close to the communities they serve. Critically evaluate India's experience with decentralised planning and suggest measures to bridge the gap between policy intent and ground-level outcomes.
Panchayati Raj: Constitutional Mandate vs. Institutional Reality

India's urban governance faces a crisis of institutional capacity, with Urban Local Bodies lacking functional autonomy, financial resources, and professional expertise. In the context of the conclusion of the Smart Cities Mission, discuss the reforms needed to make urban governance more effective and responsive.
Urban Local Bodies: 74th Amendment and the Challenge of Urban Self-Governance

sections.t: 8. Welfare Schemes for Vulnerable Sections & Their Performance

qs.q: The formal recognition of gig and platform workers under the Code on Social Security, 2020 is a landmark step, yet its promise remains largely unrealised. Discuss the challenges in extending meaningful social security to India's platform workforce.

The rapid expansion of the platform economy has created a new class of gig and platform workers who lack traditional employment protections. Examine the challenges faced by gig workers in India and evaluate the adequacy of existing legal and policy frameworks in ensuring their social security and welfare.
Social Security Architecture in India and the Case for Universal Income Guarantee

With the extension of health assurance to all senior citizens aged seventy and above, India has moved closer to universal health coverage. Assess the preparedness of the public health system to meet the needs of an ageing population.

India's rapidly ageing population presents unprecedented challenges for social protection, healthcare, and the dignity of senior citizens. Discuss the adequacy of India's policy framework for elderly welfare and suggest measures to prepare for the demographic transition.

Despite constitutional safeguards and targeted welfare schemes, India's tribal communities continue to face disproportionate deprivation in terms of health, education, and livelihood security. Examine the effectiveness of government interventions for tribal welfare with special reference to the Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups (PVTGs).

India's National Food Security Act, 2013, entitles nearly two-thirds of the population to subsidised foodgrains, yet food insecurity and nutritional deprivation persist. Evaluate the effectiveness of the NFSA and the Public Distribution System in ensuring food and nutrition security, and suggest reforms for improving targeting and delivery.
PMAY-Urban 2.0: Affordable Housing and Structural Challenges

sections.t: 9. Mechanisms, Laws, Institutions & Bodies for Protection of Vulnerable Sections

qs.q: The reservation of seats for women in legislatures, once operational, must be accompanied by measures that address the deeper structural barriers to women's political participation. Comment.
